commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: Hebrew, "and Agag came to him delicately. "Septuagint, "trembling "(Haydock) or walking with a soft step, or "with bands or chains "mahadannoth. (Menochius) Some think that he presented himself boldly, like a king, fearing nothing. (Vatable) Manner. Hebrew, "Surely the bitterness of death is past. "I have obtained pardon from Saul. But the sense of the Vulgate seems preferable, as he must have perceived, from the looks of the prophet, that death was hanging over him. Hence others translate, "is pouring upon me "instead of, is past. Septuagint, "Is death thus bitter. "Chaldean, "I pray my Lord: the bitterness of death. "(Haydock) O death. how bitter is the remembrance of thee to a man that hath peace in his possessions, Ecclesiasticus xli. So Aristotle (Nicom. ) says, "Death is most terrible, (peras gar) for it is a passage "or separation, from all the things which could attach a man to this world. (Calmet) This catastrophe of Agag and Saul, had been long before predicted, Numbers xxiv.

methodology.step_1: Parse main page with BeautifulSoup for structure
methodology.step_3: Use Firecrawl to get full content from 'Go to Commentary' links
methodology.step_4: Fallback to BeautifulSoup for accordion content if needed
advantages: Records verses even without commentaries for complete tracking
